Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Black background with two cross/plus shapes: a yellow one (left, ~row 27, col 21) and a blue one (right, ~row 45, col 36) - 8 gray 3x3 squares scattered around: 4 with yellow center dots, 4 with blue center dots - White dot at center of blue cross (~row 45, col 36) - possibly the player cursor - Purple bar at bottom row (64 pixels) - likely HUD/timer - Valid actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE
- Goal model: - Move white dot to gray squares to collect colored dots
- Action model: - SPACE: toggle cursor between cross centers - UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT: move white dot along cross arms
- Plan: - Try UP again to continue moving up and see if I can reach a gray square
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after second UP
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before.segmentation['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after.segmentation['nodes']}

print("=== White dot positions ===")
for n in before.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  Before: node {n['id']}, center=({sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)},{sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)})")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  After:  node {n['id']}, center=({sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b)},{sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b)})")

print("\n=== All small nodes after ===")
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 13:
        b = n['boundary']
        center_r = sum(p[0] for p in b) // len(b)
        center_c = sum(p[1] for p in b) // len(b)
        print(f"  Node {n['id']:2d}: color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']:3d}, center=({center_r:2d},{center_c:2d})")

# Show ascii of cross area
ascii_lines = after.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Cross area ===")
for i in range(15, 30):
    print(f"Row {i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][14:30]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern more clearly:
- White dot was at (24, 21), moved to (21, 21) - another 3 rows UP
- The horizontal bar of the cross is now at row 21 (was row 24)
- The cross is moving UP by 3 rows each time
- The gray square at (17, 15) is still there
- Two new gray squares appeared at (24, 40) and (24, 53) with blue dots - these are the positions where blue-dotted squares used to be, but they seem to have been left behind as the cross moved
Wait, let me look more carefully. The nodes at (24, 40) and (24, 53) are now gray squares with blue dots (nodes 16, 18 and 17, 19). But these positions were where blue-dotted gray squares already existed in the initial state (nodes 12 and 13). So these aren't new - they were already there.
Actually wait, looking at the initial state:
- Node 12: c, 8px, center (24, 40) with node 14: b, 1px
- Node 13: c, 8px, center (24, 53) with node 15: b, 1px
After the second UP:
- Node 16: c, 8px, center (24, 40) with node 18: b, 1px
- Node 17: c, 8px, center (24, 53) with node 19: b, 1px
So the same gray squares with blue dots are still there. The node IDs changed but the positions are the same.
OK so the pattern is:
1. Each UP moves the white dot and horizontal bar UP by 3 rows
2. The vertical arm stays in place (col 21)
3. The cross is essentially sliding up
Let me think about what the goal might be. The gray squares with yellow dots are at:
- (3, 15)
- (9, 6)
- (9, 24)
- (17, 15)
The white dot is at (21, 21). If I keep going UP, the horizontal bar will eventually reach row 18, then 15, then 12, etc. The gray square at (3, 15) has a yellow dot. If the horizontal bar reaches row 3 and the white dot reaches (3, 21), it won't be at the same position as the gray square at (3, 15).
But wait - the gray square at (17, 15) is at column 15, and the horizontal bar extends to about column 14-28. So when the horizontal bar is at row 17, the white dot would be at (17, 21) and the gray square is at (17, 15). They're on the same row but different columns.
Hmm, maybe I need to move LEFT/RIGHT along the horizontal bar to reach the gray squares? Let me try moving LEFT from the current position.
Actually, let me first understand the full movement pattern. The white dot is at (21, 21). The gray square at (17, 15) is 4 rows up and 6 columns left. If I move UP once more, the white dot will be at (18, 21). Then if I move LEFT, maybe it will move along the horizontal bar.
Let me try LEFT to see what happens.
